segments.s086-g25: بروی دژم is an adverbial phrase meaning “with a grim/sullen face” (بروی = on/at the face; دژم = grim/sullen). گفت is the past verb “said” and با + بارمان marks the addressee (“to/with Barman”). Word order is typical Persian: adverbial phrase + verb + preposition + addressee.
segments.s086-g26: که introduces reported speech/clause “that …”; جوشن بپوش is an imperative/instructional infinitive construction: جوشن (cuirass/armor) + بپوش (put on). و links the two imperatives; به زه کن کمان is another verbal command where به + زه (to/the bow‑string) + کن (do/make — here: string) + کمان (bow) — overall: “put on the cuirass and string the bow.”
segments.s086-g27: تو باشی is an exhortation/imperative sense “you be / you remain” (باشی here = remain/stand). بران انجمن = “in/at that assembly” (بران = there/at that; انجمن = assembly). سرفراز is an adjectival complement meaning “proud/victorious,” describing the addressee’s state while remaining there.
segments.s086-g28: This clause expresses protection/no harm: به انگشت دندان نیاید به گاز is literal “a tooth shall not come to (your) finger by a bite.” به انگشت = toward the finger; دندان نیاید = “a tooth will not come” (نیاید = will not come/appear); به گاز = “by a bite” (گاز = bite). The phrasing is idiomatic/pictorial: ‘no bite will reach the finger.’
segments.s086-g29: بشد here is an archaic/poetic past/inchoative meaning “he set out / it came about / he went”; بارمان is the personal name (Barman) as subject. تا … دشت نبرد uses تا = “as far as / to” + به دشت (to the plain) + نبرد (battle — here the plain where battle occurs), so the phrase means he moved/advanced as far as the battlefield/plain.
segments.s086-g30: سوی marks direction/target (“toward”); قارن and کاوه are personal names (Qaren and Kaveh). آواز کرد is a light-verb construction where آواز (call/voice) + کرد (did/made) = “he called (out).” Word order: directional phrase + addressees + verbal phrase.
segments.s086-g31: کزین is an old/formal prepositional compound meaning “from this / of this.” لشکر نوذر نامدار is noun + proper name + adjective: “the army of Nowzar, famed/renowned” (نامدار follows the noun as a descriptive epithet). The whole phrase establishes source/possession: ‘of this renowned army of Nowzar.’
segments.s086-g32: This is a two‑level relative/interrogative clause: the first که introduces the object/relative (“which/whom you have”); داری = “you have/possess.” The second که introduces the subordinate content “that/who with me کند کارزار” where کند (3rd‑person sing. of کردن) = “does/makes” and کارزار = “battle.” Overall: “Which (men) do you have who will fight me?”
segments.s086-g33: نگه کرد is a past-tense verbal phrase (literally 'made a look' = 'looked'); the verb comes before the subject (قارن) in this poetic order. به marks the target of the gaze ('at'); مردان مرد is an intensifying noun phrase ('men' + 'man/valiant man') meaning 'valiant men' or 'heroes'.
segments.s086-g34: ازان = archaic from/’of that’ (از آن); تا که here introduces purpose/extent meaning 'so as to / in order that' (i.e. 'to see'); جوید is present/simple literary form 'seeks' or 'searches for'; نبرد is the object 'battle'. The clause as a whole means 'from that assembly, so as to see who seeks battle.'
segments.s086-g35: کس here functions in a negative context as 'no one'; از نامدارانش ('from his notables') marks membership ('of his famed men'). پاسخ نداد is the negative past construction 'did not give an answer' = 'did not answer.'
segments.s086-g36: مگر introduces an exception ('except'). پیرگشته is a past-participial adjective 'having become old / aged'; دلاور is an epithet 'valiant/brave'; قباد is the proper name at the end. The phrase means 'except the aged, valiant Qobad.'
segments.s086-g37: دژم گشت is 'became sullen/grim' (verb after adjective); سالار is the subject 'the commander/chief.' بسیار هوش is a verbless predicate meaning 'very aware/alert' (بسیار modifies the noun هوش 'awareness').
segments.s086-g38: ز (poetic) = 'from/at' introduces the cause: 'from his brother's words' (گفت برادر = 'the brother's words'). برآمد is an intransitive past 'arose/sprang up'; به جوش is a locution meaning 'into zeal/ardor' i.e. 'in a fury.'
segments.s086-g39: ز خشمش = 'from/because of his anger' (خشم + possessive -ش). سرشک is a poetic word for 'tear( drop)'; اندر آمد is 'came in' (arrived into); به چشم gives the location 'into the eye.' The phrase describes a tear forming because of anger.
segments.s086-g40: از آن لشکر = 'from/with regard to that army'; گشن here is the noun 'hunger'; بد جای (بدِ جای) = 'a bad substitute/instead of' (جای 'in place of'); خشم = 'anger.' Together: 'for that army, hunger was a poor substitute for anger.'
segments.s086-g73: 'سپار‌ای' is سپار (imperative of سپردن, 'entrust') with the vocative ای attached to address someone ('O ...'); thus 'سپار ای برادر' = 'I/leave/entrust (this), O brother'. The clause 'تو پدرود باش' is an imperative instruction: تو is the stressed subject pronoun 'you' and پدرود (an archaic/poetic noun meaning 'farewell') is the predicate; باش is the imperative 'be'. Together it commands the brother to act as the speaker's farewell. The two parts are linked: the speaker entrusts something to the brother and immediately orders him to be the farewell.
segments.s086-g74: همیشه (always) modifies the imperative باش that closes the line. 'خرد تار و تو پود باش' is a woven-metaphor: خرد ('wisdom') is named as تار (the warp), و is the coordinating 'and', تو ('you') is the weft (پود), and باش is the imperative 'be'. Grammatically this is an exhortation in the vocative/imperative mood telling the addressee to be intertwined with wisdom.
segments.s086-g75: بگفت is the past-tense verb meaning “he said” (the b‑ prefix is an older/perfective marker here); این is the demonstrative “this.” و links clauses “and.” بگرفت is another past verb “he seized/took hold (of).” نیزه به دست is a prepositional/locative phrase where نیزه “spear” is the object and به + دست expresses ‘into/into his hand’ or ‘in his hand.’ Together: “He said this, and seized the spear in his hand.”
segments.s086-g76: به here means “to/toward” the place; آوردگه is the destination/venue “place of bringing/arena.” رفت is simple past “he went.” چون is a simile particle “like/as.” پیل مست is a noun + adjective: پیل “elephant” and مست “drunk/intoxicated” used figuratively; the phrase means “like a drunken elephant.”
segments.s086-g77: چنین گفت is a formula “thus he said.” با here marks the addressee/companion (“to/with”) and introduces the person spoken to. رزم‌زن is a compound meaning “one who fights / warrior” (رزم “battle” + زن “one who strikes”); بارمان is the proper name. The whole unit: “Thus he said to the warrior Barman.”
segments.s086-g78: که introduces a reported clause “that.” آورد is past “brought.” پیشم is a locative “before me / in my presence” (پیش + enclitic م for ‘me’). سرت را is the object “your head” with را marking the definite object. زمان is the subject placed at the end for emphasis: “Time brought your head before me.” Word order is flexible and topicalizes زمان (Time).
segments.s086-g79: ببایست + infinitive ماندن expresses necessity/obligation: “it was necessary to remain” or “one must stay.” که introduces the causal/explanatory clause “for” or “because.” خود روزگار is emphatic: خود “itself/very” + روزگار “time/fate,” i.e. “fate itself” or “the times themselves.”
segments.s086-g80: همی کرد is the old/imperfective past construction (همی + کرد) meaning “was doing” or “kept on doing” — here “was waging.” با جان تو کارزار is an idiomatic object phrase “(a) battle with your life” — با جان تو “against your life” and کارزار “battle.” Together: “(Fate) was waging battle against your life.”
segments.s086-g89: به فرجام is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'at/to the end' fronted for emphasis. پیروز شد is the past tense copular construction (پیروز + شد) meaning 'became/was victorious.' بارمان (the personal name) appears after the verb, a typical poetic inversion where the subject is postposed for emphasis.
segments.s086-g90: به میدان جنگ is a directional phrase 'into/to the field of battle' (به + میدان + جنگ). اندر is an older/poetic locative preposition meaning 'in/into' used here alongside به to mark the setting. آمد is the past tense 'came,' and دمان is the final constituent (here functioning as the person/name who came), placed after the verb as often occurs in epic diction.
segments.s086-g91: یکی 'someone' introduces an indefinite actor. خشت 'a brick' is the direct object, and زد is the simple past 'struck/struck at.' بر is the preposition 'upon/on,' سرین is a noun meaning 'saddle' (or seat), and قباد is the proper name in the object phrase 'upon Qobad's saddle.' The clause follows the typical SOV/verb‑final shape but with the verb earlier due to poetic ordering.
segments.s086-g92: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'so that/and thus' (here linking the action to its consequence). بند functions as 'band/girdle' (a bound or fastening) modifying کمرگاه 'waist/girdle area.' او is the third‑person pronoun 'his/he,' and برگشاد is a past participial/adjectival form 'opened' (bar‑گشاد, perfective), describing the result of the blow.
segments.s086-g93: ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from/off/from the ...'; اسپ 'horse/steed' is the source. اندر آمد is the compound idea 'came in/appeared,' and نگونسار is an adjective 'mournful/woeful' qualifying سر 'head.' The clause describes the head emerging from the horse, with the adjective before the noun as is normal.
segments.s086-g94: شد is the past/copular 'became/was.' آن 'that/the' points back to the previously mentioned person; شیردل is a compound adjective 'lion‑hearted' (brave). پیر 'old' further modifies سالار 'commander/chief.' The final سر here functions figuratively as 'head/leader' (i.e., he became the chief/leader), with adjectives stacked before the noun in classical style.
segments.s086-g95: بشد is an older/poetic form meaning 'it came to be / it happened' (here introducing the change of scene). بارمان is the personal name (subject). نزد is the preposition 'in the presence of/at the court of,' and افراسیاب is the addressee/name. The phrase means 'Barman came before Afrasiab.'
segments.s086-g96: شکفته 'blossomed/flushed' modifies دو رخسار 'two cheeks' (a poetic way to say his face brightened). با means 'with,' tying the following qualities to the blossoming. جاه 'rank/status' and آب in this figurative context means 'grace/credit/splendor,' so جاه و آب together describe his dignified appearance; و is the coordinating 'and.' The sequence is a poetic description of his radiant bearing.
segments.s086-g49: بخون‌گر شود لعل، مویی سپید؛ — Poetic clause with inverted order: شود (become) is the verb taking لعل (ruby) as its subject; بخون‌گر is an adjectival/participial characterization applied to the ruby ('blood‑stained' / 'blood‑bringing'), placed before the verb for emphasis. مویی سپید is an elliptic second clause: مویی (a single hair) + سپید (white) with the copular sense supplied by the earlier شود (i.e. 'a hair becomes white').
segments.s086-g50: شوند این دلیران همه ناامید — شوند (become) is plural and predicts a change of state for the subject دلیران (brave ones). این points to the foregoing group ('these'); همه reinforces universality ('all'); ناامید is a predicate adjective ('hopeless'). Word order is subject after the verb for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.s086-g51: شکست اندرآید بدین رزم‌گاه — شکست (defeat) is the grammatical subject; اندرآید is an older/poetic present/subjunctive form meaning 'befall' or 'occurs'. بدین رزم‌گاه is a locative phrase 'in/at this battlefield' (بدین = ب + این); the prefix ب marks 'in/at' and رزم‌گاه is the place 'battlefield'. Note the compound بدین is split in the word list but functions as one locative unit.
segments.s086-g52: پر از درد گردد دل نیک‌خواه — گرد (here گردد/gشت) is a verb meaning 'become'; دل نیک‌خواه ('the well‑wisher's heart') is the subject that changes state. پر از درد is a fixed prepositional phrase 'full of pain' (پر = 'full' + از = 'of' + درد = 'pain'), so the whole means 'the well‑wisher's heart becomes full of pain.'
segments.s086-g53: نگه کن که با قارن رزم زن! — نگه کن is an imperative 'look/watch!' or 'heed!'. که introduces the content clause that follows. با قارن is 'with Qāran' (با = 'with'); قارن here functions as the named companion/hero. رزم زن is an imperative compound: رزم (battle) + زن (imperative of زدن 'strike/do') = 'wage battle' or 'fight!' directed at the addressee together with قارن.
segments.s086-g54: چه گوید قباد اندران انجمن: — This is a question/lead‑in: چه 'what' + (می)گوید 'does [he] say' (here the line uses the noun-form/archaic presentation of speech), قباد is the speaker's name, and اندران انجمن = 'in that assembly' (اندران = 'among/therein', انجمن = 'assembly/meeting'). The clause sets up Qobad's forthcoming words.
segments.s086-g55: «بدان‌ای برادر که تن مرگ راست — بدان‌ای is an emphatic imperative 'know this' addressed to برادر 'O brother'. که introduces the content clause. تن مرگ راست is a verbless predication typical of Persian poetry: 'the body — death — is certain' (راست = 'true/sure'), i.e. 'know, brother, that death for the body is certain.'
segments.s086-g56: سر رزم زن سودن ترگ راست — Poetic clause with compressed word order: سر رزم‌زن = 'the head of the warrior/in battle (rider‑fighter)'; سودن (سودن) is the verb 'to avail/be of use'; ترگ ('a shred'/'rag') is the thing to which it is compared; راست closes the sentence with 'truly/indeed.' The sense is a bleak proverb: even a warrior's head in battle avails only like a shred — i.e. little or no lasting gain.
segments.s086-g57: ز + گاه = a prepositional phrase 'from (the) time/era'; خجسته is an adjective modifying گاه ('auspicious/blessed'); منوچهر is the proper name in genitive relation ('the time of Manuchehr'); باز is an adverb 'again' placed after the name for emphasis. The whole segment functions as a temporal adverbial 'since the auspicious time of Manuchehr, again...'.
segments.s086-g58: از امروز is a second temporal phrase 'from/today'; بودم is the first-person past of 'to be' ('I was / I have been') and here introduces the predication; تن اندر گداز is a nominal predicate 'body in melting/anguish' with اندر = 'in/within' and گداز used metaphorically as 'torment/ardor'. Word order is slightly poetic: temporal phrase + verb (بودم) + predicate noun phrase.
segments.s086-g59: کسی here with a negative predicate gives the sense 'no one'; زنده is a postpositive adjective 'alive'; بر آسمان is a prepositional phrase 'onto/into the sky (heaven)'; نگذرد is the present/gnomic negative of گذریدن 'to pass', so the clause means 'no living one passes to heaven' (i.e. none escape or go to heaven).
segments.s086-g60: شکارست is a predicative compound meaning 'is prey' (شکار + -ست copula). و links clauses. مرگش is 'his death' with the enclitic -ش. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/surely' here) and بشکرد is an archaic verb meaning 'to come to pass' (used here to mean 'his death will occur'). The clause states a certainty about the person's being prey and dying.
segments.s086-g61: یکی را uses را to mark the affected person ('someone' as patient). برآید (here impersonal/third-person) means 'will befall/come about' or 'is taken' and governs the following instrument phrase. به شمشیر indicates the instrument ('by the sword'), and هوش is the thing affected ('sense/consciousness'); together the phrase means 'someone's senses are taken away by the sword' (i.e. struck senseless).
segments.s086-g62: بدانگه is an adverbial 'at that moment/thereupon'; که introduces a subordinate clause 'when/that'; آید is third-person present 'come' (here 'when ... come'); دو لشگر is the subject 'two armies'; به جوش is a prepositional phrase 'into a boil/surge' meaning 'in fury/agitation'. The whole subordinate clause gives the time: 'when two armies come in upheaval.'
segments.s086-g63: تنش is 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش). The nouns کرگس و شیر درنده are conjoined predicates/images describing the body ('a vulture and a ferocious lion'). راست at the end is a poetic predicate particle roughly equivalent to 'is indeed' or 'is straight/true', completing the equational assertion. The phrase is metaphorical: 'his body is like a vulture and a fierce lion.'
segments.s086-g64: سرش 'his head' (سر + ش) is followed by a list of weapons نیزه و تیغ برنده ('spear and a sharp sword') where برنده modifies تیغ. راست again functions as the copular/affirmative particle. The clause thus says 'his head (is) spear and a keen sword,' i.e. his head bears/has a spear and a sharp blade — a compact, poetic way to indicate martial armament.
segments.s086-g65: This is a poetic, inverted temporal clause: زمان ('time') is the governing noun, and the verb برآید ('comes/arises') governs the subordinate phrase 'یکی را به بستر برآید' meaning 'someone is brought to the bed' — i.e. 'when it is someone’s time to go to the bed/deathbed.' را is the object/topic marker attached to یکی (an indefinite person) and به + بستر gives the goal 'to/into the bed.' Word order is topicalized for meter (object/phrase appears before زمان).
segments.s086-g66: همی is an older emphatic/aspectual particle (here stressing the certainty/continuity of the action). باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary' and رفت is the lexical verb 'go/leave' (here with the sense 'depart'); the order همی رفت باید is a poetically altered order of the idiom باید رفت ('one must go'), placing the verb and particle before the modal for rhythm. ز بن = 'from the root/foundation' and بی‌گمان is an adverbial 'undoubtedly.'
segments.s086-g67: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. من is the emphatic personal pronoun; روم here is a classical subjunctive/optative form 'I go / I depart' (i.e. 'if I were to leave / die'). زین is the fused/prepositional demonstrative 'from this' (classical ز + این), and جهان فراخ is a noun + adjective 'this spacious/wide world.'
segments.s086-g68: This is a direct address 'برادر' (brother). به جایست is an impersonal/fixed predicate meaning 'it is fitting/proper (for you) to be in the place' (جایست here = 'it is fitting' rather than literal 'place'). با + برز و شاخ lists qualities/attributes 'with loftiness and horn' (برز 'height, exaltation'; شاخ here literal 'horn' used metaphorically for might). The implied subject of جایست is understood as 'you' (the addressed brother).
segments.s086-g69: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'someone / one (person)'. دخمه خسروانی is a genitival compound 'a royal tomb' (دخمه = tomb, خسروانی = royal/Khosrowian). کند is the 3rd-person present/jussive 'let him do / let someone do' — a common classical Persian way to give an imperative wish by using کند with an indefinite subject.
segments.s086-g70: پس از = 'after' (پس used here with از to mark temporal sequence). رفتنم is the verbal noun/gerund 'my going / my departure' (contextually 'my death' or 'after I am gone'). مهربانی is the noun 'kindness/mercy' and کند again is the 3rd-person present/jussive 'do / show' (i.e. 'show kindness after I'm gone'). The clause is a request: after my departure, perform an act of kindness for me.
segments.s086-g71: سرم را is 'my head' with the definite/object marker را. به introduces the materials/instrument ('with' or 'in' in classical usage) and is followed by a series of substances (کافور, مشک, گلاب) linked by و. The whole phrase is an elliptical request: '(place) my head in/with camphor, musk and rosewater.'
segments.s086-g72: تنم را 'my body' (را marks it as the object/topic). بدان is classical fused preposition/pronoun (بـ + آن) meaning 'into/into that' or 'in that' — here introducing the destination 'that place.' جای جاوید is a noun 'the eternal place' (جای + adjective جاوید 'eternal'), and خواب 'sleep' names the state (i.e. 'eternal sleep'); the verb is omitted (elliptical), so the phrase functions as a jussive/request: 'let my body be placed into that eternal sleep.'
segments.s086-g41: ز introduces the following noun phrase with the sense 'of/from' in poetic register. چِندان (preceding the adjective جوان) means 'many/so much' and modifies جوان (young). مردم جنگجوی is a compound nominal phrase 'people/folk who are warriors' (i.e. 'warriors'); the poetic word order places modifiers before the head noun.
segments.s086-g42: یکی پیر forms the subject 'one old man' (یکی = 'someone/one', پیر = 'old'). جوید is 3rd-person singular present ('seeks') and همی is the archaic/prosaic continuous/emphatic marker ('still/continually') accompanying the verb. رزم اوی is the verb's object: رزم 'battle/contest' with the enclitic اوی referring to 'him' (third person); the phrase therefore means 'seeks the battle with him' (= 'seeks to fight him').
segments.s086-g43: دل قارن is a possessive/identifying sequence meaning 'Qaran's heart' (name follows noun in poetic order). آزرده گشت is a two-word predication: آزرده 'distressed' + گشت 'became' (past), so 'became distressed.' از here marks cause/agent 'because of' (here 'because of Qobad').
segments.s086-g44: میان دلیران is a prepositional phrase 'among the brave/valiant ones.' زبان برگشاد is a semi-idiomatic collocation: زبان 'speech/tongue' + برگشاد 'opened' (past), literally 'opened his tongue' = 'he spoke up.' The verb برگشاد (بر + گشاد) is a past form meaning 'opened'.
segments.s086-g45: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). سال تو here means 'your years/age' (سال used as 'age/period'); اکنون 'now.' به جایی رسید is an idiom 'has reached a place/point' (به + جایی + رسید), i.e. 'has come to a point/stage.' The verb رسید is past ('reached').
segments.s086-g46: که again introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. از جنگ marks the domain/source 'from war' (i.e. 'in regard to fighting'). دستت is 'your hand' (دست + enclitic ت = 'your hand'); in this idiom دست کشیدن از جنگ literally 'to pull/withdraw one's hand from war' = 'to desist from fighting.' بباید is the modal 'it is necessary / must,' and کشید (here used after بباید) functions as the verbal action 'to pull/withdraw' (older/poetic use where the bare past-form follows the modal).
segments.s086-g47: تویی is emphatic 'thou art' / 'you are.' مایه‌ور is a predicate adjective 'wealthy' (literally 'rich in means'), and کدخدای سپاه is a titulary/genitive phrase 'the kethuda (headman) of the army' where کدخدای is the headman/title and سپاه ('army') is the genitive. The whole segment is a predicate: 'You are a wealthy kethuda of the army.'
segments.s086-g48: همی is the archaic/emphatic present marker here ('will/shall' or 'indeed'), بر تو گردد is an impersonal/optative construction 'turn upon you / fall to you' (گردد = 'become/happen' in 3rd singular), and همه رای شاه is the noun phrase 'all the king's counsel/decisions' (همه 'all' + رای 'counsel/plan' + شاه 'king'). The line means 'all the king's counsel/decisions will be directed at you.'
segments.s086-g113: This clause is a temporal subordinate introduced by چو ('when'). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' placed before the name Qaren (قارن) for emphasis; the verb بدید (past) goes at the end, meaning 'he saw.' The sequence literally reads 'from Qaren when Afrasiab that saw' → 'when Afrasiab saw Qaren (thus/that).' Note the older/poetic order (preposition + name before چو + subject + past verb).
segments.s086-g114: Two coordinated actions: بزد (past) + direct object اسپ (horse) — here بزد means 'struck' (often idiomatically 'spurred/struck his horse') — followed by و linking the second clause, in which لشکر (subject) + سوی او (prepositional phrase 'toward him') precede the verb کشید. In classical poetry the object/order can be rearranged for rhythm: 'he struck his horse, and he drew the army toward him.'
segments.s086-g115: یکی رزم is 'a certain/one battle' (یکی = a certain), followed by the time phrase تا شب 'until night.' برآمد (past) is the verb 'arose/rose up,' and ز کوه is the source 'from the mountain.' Word order is subject/time/verb/source typical of epic diction.
segments.s086-g116: This is two coordinated parts: بکردند (they did/made) = 'they engaged/ fought,' joined by و. نامد here functions as a noun 'fame' or 'renown' that follows that action, and the second half 'دل از کین ستوه' is a verbless predicative phrase meaning 'the heart, weary from hatred' (دل = heart, از = from, کین = vengeance/hatred, ستوه = weary). Classical Persian often omits a copula, so read as 'and fame came; the heart was weary from hatred.'
segments.s086-g117: Chained temporal clause: چو شب تیره شد = 'when the night became dark' (چو = when; شد = became). The subject (قارن) and his epithet رزمخواه ('war-desiring' / eager for battle) follow; the adjective functions attributively. The verb for the action appears in the next segment.
segments.s086-g118: بیاورد is a past-tense verb 'he brought' (here: 'he brought/led'), followed by سوی دهستان ('toward the district/village territory') and the direct object سپاه (army). Poetic word order places the destination before the object: 'brought toward the district the army.'
segments.s086-g119: بر نوذر آمد is a short clause 'he came to/onto Nowzar' — بر + proper name + آمد gives the sense 'approached/came upon Nowzar.' به پرده سرای is a locative phrase 'into the curtained chamber' (پرده = curtain, سرای = chamber/abode); be + noun marks motion into. In epic Persian the sequence 'came to [person] into [place]' is normal.
segments.s086-g120: This is a verbless resultive clause: ز خون برادر شده دل ز جای = 'the heart, from the brother's blood, became (shaken) from its place.' ز خون برادر = 'because of/of the brother's blood' (ز = from/because of, خون = blood, برادر = brother). شده is the past participle 'become/been made,' and دل ز جای literally 'the heart from its place' = 'the heart was dislodged/moved.'
segments.s086-g105: به گرد اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning “around the interior/within”; به + گرد (here 'گرد' = around) precedes the locative noun اندرون (an archaic word for 'inside'). همچو دریای آب is a simile: همچو 'like' + دریای آب ('sea of water', دریای being the construct form of دریا + آب as its qualifier). Word order is poetic but standard: preposition/locative → simile phrase.
segments.s086-g106: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'دریای آب' ("which/that"). شنگرف is the noun subject of بارد (here from باریدن, present/past-poetic 'pours' or 'falls'), and بْرو آفتاب means 'toward the sun' (برو = به رو/برُو 'toward' + آفتاب 'sun'); the verb بارد is in a classical/poetic form. Note some words here are archaic/poetic usages rather than their literal modern-listed senses.
segments.s086-g107: پر از + N means 'full of N' (پر here functions adjectivally 'full'). The construction places the predicate قبل از شد, so: 'پر از نالهِ کوس شد مغزِ میغ' = 'the core of the blade became full of the drum's lament.' شد is the past/copular verb 'became' and مغز میغ (core of the blade) is the grammatical subject placed at the end for poetic emphasis.
segments.s086-g108: Similar structure: پر از آب شنگرف شد جان تیغ = 'the sword's life became full of the water of shengarf.' Again پر از expresses 'filled with', شد is 'became', and جان تیغ ('the life/soul of the sword') is the subject in final position. The phrase آب شنگرف is a poetic compound 'water of the glitter/shine.'
segments.s086-g109: به هر سو = 'to every side/whithersoever'; که introduces a relative clause 'that/where'; قارن here is a proper name (Qaran) and is the agent of the verb برافگند, the past form of افکندن ('threw/dispatched'). اسپ is the object 'steed/horse'. Word order is poetic: locative → relative clause → agent → verb → object.
segments.s086-g110: همی تافت is a classical past/perfective phrase meaning 'shone' with همی adding a sense of continuous or emphatic action. آهن here refers poetically to weapon-metal/steel (not merely raw iron) and چو is the simile 'like/as'. آذرگشسپ is a proper name (a famed hero), so the iron's shining is compared to that hero's (or his famed weapon's) brilliance.
segments.s086-g111: تو گفتی که introduces reported speech: 'you said that ...'. الماس مرجان فشاند is a straightforward clause in which الماس (diamond) is the subject, مرجان (coral) the object, and فشاند (from فشاندن) is the past tense 'scattered/sprinkled.' Note: گفتی is the 2nd-person past of 'to say' (reported here), though the provided sense list did not include the verb sense.
segments.s086-g112: چه at the start of an exclamative clause ('what!' or 'how!') questions the worth or reality of the preceding image. The relative clause که ... فشاند qualifies/contrasts that image: 'what (use is) coral, when in كین (vengeance/hatred) it pours forth life.' همی here marks the ongoing/continued action (classical usage) and فشاند again is 'casts/sends forth.' The clause overturns the earlier glittering image by invoking deadly vengeance.
segments.s086-g17: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so” that prefaces reported speech. گفت is the simple past verb “said” (3rd person), placed before the speaker’s name in this poetic order (verb before subject). اغریرث is a proper name; هوشمند is an adjective “wise/intelligent” modifying the name and follows it (name + epithet).
segments.s086-g18: که‌گر (= که گر) is an archaic conditional conjunction meaning “if.” بارمان را: بارمان is the person Barman and را is the definite/object marker marking him as the affected party. رسد is a 3rd-person verb here meaning “befall / come to” (it conveys happening to someone). زین گزند is a compact, slightly archaic phrase meaning “from this harm/this injury” (زین functions as a poetic demonstrative ‘this’ followed by گزند “harm”). Together the clause says “if this harm befalls Barman.”
segments.s086-g19: دل مرزبانان is a genitive construction: دل “heart” of مرزبانان “the frontier-guards” (the possessor follows the possessed). شکسته شود is a passive/periphrastic result phrasing: شکسته (past participle “broken”) + شود (3rd-person present/subjunctive/optative auxiliary), giving the sense “will be broken / will become broken.” Word order is typical poetic: possessed + possessor, then predicate.
segments.s086-g20: برین is the fused form of بر + این (“on/upon this” or “in this”), where ین is the older form of این. انجمن کار = “the assembly’s business/affairs” (انجمن “assembly”, کار “affair”). بسته شود repeats the past-participle + شود pattern (“be closed / be ended”). The whole clause means “upon this assembly the affairs will be closed.”
segments.s086-g21: یکی here is an indefinite “a certain / one (person).” مرد بی‌نام is the noun phrase “a nameless man.” باید + گزید is the modal construction: باید (must/ought) followed by گزید (past stem of گزیدن) yields the obligation “must choose / one must choose” — in context an instruction to select such a man.
segments.s086-g22: که introduces the consequence or purpose “so that / such that.” انگشت literally “finger” but here evokes the idiom of pointing/blaming; ازان پس = از + آن + پس (“from that, thereafter” = thereafter). نباید گزید is a negative modal construction: نباید (should not / must not) + گزید (here contextually ‘to point/choose’ — i.e. to single someone out). The clause means roughly “so that afterwards no finger should be pointed (no one may be accused).”
segments.s086-g23: پرآژنگ is a poetic adjective describing a change of color/complexion (here “flushed / crimsoned / aflame”); this is an elevated poetic term. شد is the auxiliary “became”; روی پور پشنگ is the subject phrase “the face of Pūr Pashang” (پور پشنگ is the personal name, with پور as the name element). Word order places the predicate before the subject in this line of verse.
segments.s086-g24: ز گفتار اغریرث is a prepositional phrase “from the speech/words of Aghrirath” (ز = from, گفتار = speech, اغریرث the speaker). آمدش is آمد + ـش (came + to him), a compact way to say “came upon him / befell him.” ننگ is the thing that came (shame). The literal order “from Aghrirath’s speech shame came to him” corresponds idiomatically to “he was shamed by Aghrirath’s words.”
segments.s086-g97: یکی خلعتش داد کاندر جهان: 'یکی' is the numeral/adverb 'one' or 'a single'; 'خلعتش' is 'خلعت' (robe of honour) with the enclitic -ش marking the robe as his/object; 'داد' is the past tense 'gave'. 'کاندر جهان' is a locative/adverbial phrase 'in the world' or 'such that in the world', linking this giving to a following clause describing its uniqueness.
segments.s086-g98: کس از کهتران نستد آن از مهان: 'کس' here functions as a negative indefinite 'no one'; 'از کهتران' = 'from/out of the lower-born' (source phrase). 'نستد' is the past verb (negative-prefix + ستد) used here to mean 'was able to seize / could take' (i.e. 'did not seize/could not take'). 'آن از مهان' refers back to 'that [robe] from the great/nobles' — overall: 'no one of the low-born could take that from the great.'
segments.s086-g99: چو او کشته شد قارن رزمجوی: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause 'when'; 'او کشته شد' = passive construction 'he was killed' (کشته + شد as auxiliary). 'قارن' is a personal name followed by the epithet 'رزمجوی' (postpositive epithet 'warrior/combatant'), so the main subject is 'Qaran, the warrior.'
segments.s086-g100: سپه را بیاورد و بنهاد روی: 'سپه را بیاورد' = 'he brought/led the army' with 'را' marking the definite object; 'بیاورد' is past tense 'brought/led'. 'و بنهاد روی' = 'and he set/placed (his) face' — 'بنهاد' past 'put/placed' and 'روی' = 'face' used idiomatically ('set his face' = turned/advanced).
segments.s086-g101: دو لشکر به کردار دریای چین: 'دو لشکر' = 'two armies/hosts'. 'به کردار' is a simile marker 'in the manner of / like'. 'دریای چین' is a poetic compound 'sea of folds/wrinkles' (i.e. a rippling or heaving sea) — the whole phrase is a simile comparing the two armies to a heaving sea.
segments.s086-g102: تو گفتی که شد جنب جنبان زمین: 'تو' = 'you' (address). 'گفتی' would normally be 'you said' introducing reported speech; here 'که' introduces the subordinate clause. 'شد' is a past auxiliary 'became/was' and 'جنب جنبان' is a reduplicated verbal/adjectival pair meaning 'moved and shook' (intensive). 'زمین' is the subject of that subordinate clause ('the earth'). Grammatically: 'You said that the earth became moving/shaking.'
segments.s086-g103: درخشیدن تیغ الماس گون: 'درخشیدن' is the verbal noun 'the shining/glitter'. 'تیغ' (sword) is the noun being described, and 'الماس گون' is a postpositive adjective 'diamond-like' (الماس 'diamond' + گون forming '-like'). The phrase reads 'the shining of the diamond-like sword.'
segments.s086-g104: شده لعل و آهار داده به خون: 'شده' is the perfect/past participle 'has become'; 'لعل' = 'ruby-colored' (metaphorical color). 'و آهار داده' is coordinated: 'and (its) sheen/finish has been given' — 'داده' past participle 'given'. 'به خون' indicates the medium/agent ('to/with blood'), i.e. the sheen/colour is imparted by blood. Overall: 'has become ruby, and its sheen has been given to the blood' (i.e. the sword is blood-stained and ruby-hued).
segments.s086-g81: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus' and introduces reported speech. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (reported speech) but its sense options in the list don't include 'said' so it's marked -1. مر is the old/poetic form for 'me' (first-person object). بارمان را is the name 'Barman' plus the object/definite marker را (here functioning to mark the addressee/object), and قباد is the speaker's name placed after the clause in poetic word order: 'Thus Qobad said to me, Barman...'.
segments.s086-g82: که is the subordinating particle 'that' introducing the content of the speech. یکچند means 'for a while' / 'for some time'. گیتی here means 'the world' (i.e. the course of fortune). م in the list appears unrelated to the pronoun 'مرا' and the listed senses don't fit the context, so it's marked -1 (the line functions as 'm(e)ra' = 'me' with را split). را is the object marker attached to 'مرا' in practice. داد داد are two occurrences of the verb داد 'gave' (poetic repetition/emphasis): both are used as 'gave/bestowed'.
segments.s086-g83: به جایی is a prepositional phrase 'to/for a place'. توان is the modal verb 'can' (ability). مرد is the noun 'man' (the possible actor). کاید زمان is a noun phrase 'time's contrivance/plot' (کاید 'plot/cunning' modifying زمان 'time'); word order here is compact and poetic: 'to a place a man can (go) — (when) time's contrivance (is at work)'.
segments.s086-g84: بیاید is a present/subjunctive/impersonal form 'may come' / 'will come' used here as 'the time will come'. The repetition زمان ... زمان ('time — a time') is emphatic/poetic: the first زمان is the subject and the second is the indefinite 'a time/once'. بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' reinforcing certainty.
segments.s086-g85: بگفت is the past-tense narrative 'he said' or 'he spoke'. و is the coordinating 'and'. برانگیخت is the past tense 'roused' or 'spurred up'. شبدیز را is the horse's name plus the object marker را (Shabdiz is the direct object of برانگیخت). The verb precedes the object in the poetic line but the function is standard past-tense action.
segments.s086-g86: بداد here functions as a past tense verb 'gave' (he gave). آرمیدن is an infinitive/verbal noun 'to rest' or 'resting' used as the thing given. دل تیز (literally 'heart swift/keen') is the object that receives the rest; تیز describes the heart as eager/fast. را again marks the definite object. So the clause means 'he gave rest to the eager heart.'
segments.s086-g87: ز شبگیر تا ... sets a temporal span 'from the night-watch until ...'. ز is the poetic 'from', شبگیر refers to the night-watch/dawn-period, تا is 'until', سایه گسترد is 'the shadow spread' (گسترد is the past tense 'spread'). هور at the end is the subject placed after the clause (poetic inversion) meaning 'light/splendor' or the radiant presence active over that time span.
segments.s086-g88: همی is an adverb 'continually/still'. این برآن آن برین is a compact poetic phrase meaning 'this upon that, that upon this' (mutual action/assault), with بر used as 'upon/against' and -ین an orthographic/variant form related to این. کرد زور is the past 'did/exerted force' (کرد 'did' plus زور 'force/strength'): 'force was thus at work' or 'they used strength'. The line describes continuous mutual blows/effort.
segments.s086-g1: سپیده (dawn) is personified as the grammatical subject. چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” از کوه is a simple prepositional phrase “from the mountain,” and سر برکشید is a past-tense verbal phrase (برکشید = ‘lifted/raised’) with سر (‘head/top’) as its object; the word order is literary but straightforward: temporal conjunction + prepositional phrase + verb.
segments.s086-g2: طلایه is the subject meaning “vanguard.” به پیش is a locative/directional phrase meaning “to/in front of,” with به indicating direction and پیش functioning as “before/in front of.” دهستان is the place (district/village), and رسید is the past tense of رسیدن ‘to arrive/reach’ placed at the end as the predicate.
segments.s086-g3: میان introduces a locative relation “between.” دو لشکر is the quantified noun phrase “two armies” (numeral before noun), and دو فرسنگ بود is a nominal predicate stating the distance: ‘there was two farsangs.’ بود is the past copula, giving a simple past-state meaning (“there was”).
segments.s086-g4: همه functions as an inclusive/totalizing word (‘all/the whole’). ساز و آرایش is a coordinated noun phrase ‘instruments and array/arrangement’ and جنگ qualifies that phrase as belonging to battle (‘of war’), with بود again the past copula: the whole scene consisted of instruments and the battle-array. The order is topic (whole scene) → predicate (what it was).
segments.s086-g5: یکی introduces an indefinite person ‘a certain’ or ‘one.’ ترک بد نام is a noun with a postpositive adjective: ‘an ill‑famed Turk’ (بدنام = ill‑famed/reputed badly). او بارمان is appositive: the pronoun او (‘he’) followed by the proper name بارمان (Baraman), identifying that Turk.
segments.s086-g6: همی is an epic/old poetic particle adding immediacy or iterative sense (“at once/straightaway”). خفته را uses را as the object marker meaning ‘to the sleeper’ (خفته = the one asleep). گفت is the past-tense verb ‘said’ (here introducing speech), and بیدار مان functions as the reported utterance — bیدار ‘awake’ used imperatively and مان is an uncertain/archaic element here (likely an imperative or vocative/particle). The clause thus reports him addressing the sleeper with an order to wake; the final word مان is irregular/archaic in this context.
segments.s086-g7: بیامد is the past tense ‘he came.’ سپه را uses را to mark the army as the (direct) object of the following gaze. همی بنگرید is a poetic imperfect/iterative construction: همی + present-stem بنگرید gives a habitual/continuous past sense ‘kept looking’ or ‘kept his gaze on’ the army, so the clause reads ‘he came and kept looking at the army.’
segments.s086-g8: سراپرده is a compound noun ‘inner chamber / behind the curtain’ (the king’s private tent). شاه نوذر is a genitive construction ‘the king Nowzar’ (title + proper name). بدید is the past tense ‘he saw’ (دیدن → بدید), so the clause means ‘he saw the king Nowzar’s inner tent.’
segments.s086-g129: چنین گفت قارن = literally 'thus said Qarn.' چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used to introduce reported speech). که begins the subordinate clause that follows. تا + زاده‌ام (see next) introduces a temporal condition ('as long as...').
segments.s086-g130: تن پرهنر مرگ را داده‌ام = 'I have given/dealt death to (my) skilled body.' تن ('body') is the patient; پرهنر is a compound adjective 'full of skill/skillful' following the noun. مرگ is marked by the direct-object marker را before the verb. داده‌ام is the first-person perfect (I have given/inflicted) and here carries the sense 'have doomed/caused (death)'. Word order places object+را before the verb, which is normal in Persian.
segments.s086-g131: فریدون نهاد این کله بر سرم = 'Fereydun placed/put this head upon my head.' نهاد here is the past of نهادن 'to put/place.' این کله ('this head') is the direct object; بر سرم is a prepositional phrase 'on my head' with the pronominal suffix -م 'my.' The clause reports an action done by the named subject Fereydun.
segments.s086-g132: که بر کین ایرج زمین بسپرم = 'that (I) should/ought to entrust the land to Irāj’s vengeance.' که introduces the content/purpose clause. بر + کین ایرج = 'to/into the vengeance of Irāj' (کین 'vengeance' takes the person-name بعدِ it). زمین is 'the land/earth.' بسپرم is the first-person present/subjunctive of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over,' used here to express the intended or prescribed action ('that I should hand over').
segments.s086-g133: هنوز آن کمربند نگشاده‌ام = 'I have still/yet not opened/loosened that belt.' هنوز 'still/yet' gives temporal frame; آن کمربند is the demonstrative + noun 'that belt.' نگشاده‌ام is a negated perfect/past participle construction (نـ + گشاده‌ام), here meaning 'I have not opened/loosened' — a negative perfect-form verb phrase.
segments.s086-g134: همان تیغ پولاد ننهاده‌ام = 'I have not yet put down/laid aside that very steel sword.' همان is emphatic 'that very/the same.' تیغ پولاد is 'steel sword' (noun + material). ننهاده‌ام is negated perfect of نهادن 'to place/put' (نـ + نهاده‌ام) meaning 'I have not put/placed (it).' The object (تیغ پولاد) precedes the verb, as common in Persian.
segments.s086-g135: برادر شد آن مرد سنگ و خرد = 'That man became (like) a brother, [of] stone and wisdom.' برادر شد is 'became a brother' (شد = became). آن مرد is the topicalized 'that man.' سنگ و خرد are coordinated nouns/adjectives describing him: سنگ 'stone' (metaphorically 'hard, steadfast') and خرد 'wisdom.' Persian allows this kind of juxtaposition where qualities follow the noun.
segments.s086-g136: سرانجام من هم برین بگذرد = 'In the end I too will pass away from this.' سرانجام 'finally/at last' is fronted for emphasis. من هم 'I too.' برین is orthographic contraction of بر این ('over/through this'); بگذرد is the present/subjunctive form of گذشتن used here with a future/inevitable sense ('will pass' or 'shall pass away'). The clause expresses an inevitable eventuality for the speaker.
segments.s086-g137: انوشه بدی تو is a vocative/address: the proper name انوشه with the epithet بدی, followed by the pronoun تو ('you'). که امروز جنگ begins a relative clause ('who today in the battle...') that connects to the verb in the next segment. Note the poetic word order: the relative marker که introduces the circumstance ('today, in battle').
segments.s086-g138: به تنگ اندر آورد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: به + تنگ ('into narrowness/strait') + اندر آورد (past of آورد used causatively 'brought/put into'). The verb آوَرد (3rd sg. past) takes تو/انوشه as its subject from the previous segment; پور پشنگ is the person put into distress, where پور functions here as the name element 'Pour/son of' and پشنگ is the proper name.
segments.s086-g139: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از لشکرش ('from his army') locates what follows; گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/was turned', and لختی is an adverb 'for a short while' modifying the result adjective تباه 'ruined'. The adjective follows, so literally 'when from his army it became for a little while ruined.'
segments.s086-g140: خواست is a past-tense verb here meaning 'requested/asked for'. از آسودگان ('from the at-rest' — the idle/relieved/reserve men) is the source, and چندی سپاه means 'some troops' (چندی = some, سپاه = troops/force). Word order is Subject/Agent omitted (he) + verb + object phrase.
segments.s086-g141: مرا دید is the core 'saw me' (مرا = direct object, دید = past 'saw'). با گرزه gives the instrumental 'with a mace.' گاوروی (cowherd) appears after the verb phrase by poetic inversion and functions as the subject/agent: 'the cowherd, with a mace, saw me.' Note the unusual segmentation in the manuscript (م + را), but grammatically it is the object marker on 'me.'
segments.s086-g142: بیامد is past 'came.' به نزدیکِ من is a prepositional phrase 'to/near me' (به + نزدیک + من), and جنگجوی ('warrior') names the coming person (again after the verb by poetic order). Together: 'the warrior came near me.'
segments.s086-g143: به رویش 'toward his face' (به + روی + ـش 'his'), بران گونه = 'in that way/that manner' (بران = 'upon that / in that'), and اندر شدم is an archaic past construction meaning 'I went in / I entered / I became [in that posture].' Together they express 'I approached his face in that manner.'
segments.s086-g144: که introduces the result/clarifying clause 'so that.' با دیدگانش = 'with his eyes' (i.e., in regard to his gaze), and برابر شدم is 'I became opposite / I stood face to face' — idiomatically 'I met his gaze' or 'I was face-to-face with him.' The verb شدم is the past of شدن used here to mark the resulting state.
segments.s086-g9: بشد is a past/cohortative verb used narratively here ('came/occurred'); its fronting creates a more poetic word order so the subject (سپاه) appears after it. نزد سالار توران is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before the commander of Turan' (نزد = 'in the presence of', سالار = 'commander', توران = the land/people of Turan).
segments.s086-g10: نشان داد is a light noun + verb combination meaning 'displayed / showed' (نشان = sign/standard; داد = gave/caused). ازان introduces the following nouns as 'of those / from that,' so لشکر و بارگاه is the thing shown: 'those troops and the palace / royal court.' The indirect object ('to whom') is implied from the previous segment.
segments.s086-g11: وزان پس is a compound adverbial meaning 'after that / from that time on' (وزان = 'from that'; پس = 'then/after'). به سالار بیدار گفت is a standard ditransitive pattern: به + indirect object ('to the commander, the watchful one') followed by گفت ('he said'). بیدار here is an epithet/adjective 'awake, alert.'
segments.s086-g12: که introduces the reported question/clause ('that / asking'), ما را with را marks 'us' as the object/topic. هنر چند is an interrogative noun phrase 'how much skill/art' (هنر = skill/art; چند = how much/how many). باید is a modal 'must / it is necessary' and نهفت is the verb 'to hide/conceal' (here following باید to express obligation): 'How much skill must we hide?'
segments.s086-g13: به دستوری شاه is an instrumental/agent phrase meaning 'by the king's command' (به + دستوری شاه; دستوری here = 'command/order'). من is the emphatic subject 'I', and شیروار is an adverbial 'all at once / suddenly / at once' placed after the subject for emphasis.
segments.s086-g14: بجویم is a first-person verbal form ('I will seek / I shall seek'); ازان is a demonstrative/prepositional 'from that' and introduces the source (انجمن = 'assembly, council'). کارزار names the object or domain related to that assembly — 'the battle / the fighting' — so the clause means 'I will seek the battle / information about the battle from that assembly.'
segments.s086-g15: ببینند is a 3rd-person plural verbal form used narratively ('they will see' / 'they see'). پیدا here is adverbial 'plainly / clearly / apparent', and ز من دستبرد is a poetic genitive/agent phrase 'an encroachment/assault from me' (ز = 'from', من = 'me', دستبرد = 'encroachment/seizure/attack').
segments.s086-g16: جز از من ('except from me') sets up the exclusion; کسی را is the (indefinite) object 'anyone' with the object marker را. نخوانند is the verb 'they will not call/summon' (here used negatively in the future/narrative sense). گرد is an adverb/particle 'around/about' that in combination with خواندن gives the sense 'to call around / summon to the assembly.' The whole means 'they will summon no one but me.'
segments.s086-g121: ورا (vora) is the object pronoun 'her/to her' placed before the verb (poetic word order). دید (past 'saw') has its subject نوذر (Nowzar) following it, another poetic inversion: 'Nowzar saw her'. فروریخت آب is a separate clause meaning 'water fell/poured down' (here 'water' = tears); the verb فروریخت is past tense 'fell/poured down' with آ ب as its noun.
segments.s086-g122: ازان is the archaic/prepositional combination 'from that' (از + آن). مژه 'eyelash' is the noun; سیرنادیده is a compound adjective/participle literally 'not‑having‑seen' (i.e. 'never seen/never known'), and خواب 'sleep' completes that participial idea: 'an eyelash that had never seen sleep' (i.e. sleepless). The whole phrase modifies the preceding 'tears poured from...'.
segments.s086-g123: چنین گفت is the formula 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here functions as 'he said' — note the lemma's senses list did not include the verbal sense). کز (archaic compound of که/از) here functions as 'since/from' introducing the time/ground: 'since the death of Sam'. سوار 'rider/horseman' is the noun that will be the thing not seen in the next clause; word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s086-g124: ندیدم is the speaker's past negative 'I did not see'. روان را uses را as the object/extension marker ('for the soul' or 'the soul' as the thing mourned), and چنین سوگوار is the predicate 'so mourning/so full of grief'. Grammatically the clause completes the idea begun in the previous segment: 'I have not seen (a) rider so mourning for the soul.'
segments.s086-g125: چو خورشید 'like the sun' (چو = 'as/like', خورشید = 'sun'). بادا is an optative particle 'may it be' (classical Persian wish/benediction). روان قباد is a possessive order 'the soul of Qobad' (possessor postposed), so the whole clause reads 'May Qobad's soul be like the sun.'
segments.s086-g126: ترا here functions as 'to you / for you' (archaic/vocative use addressing Qobad). زین جهان 'from/out of this world' (زین = contraction of ز + این in classical usage) and جاودان 'eternal' form the predicate; بهر باد is a final optative ('بهر' = 'for', used idiomatically; باد = 'may it be'). Together: 'may you be eternal beyond this world.' Note: word order and particles are classical/poetic.
segments.s086-g127: کزین = 'from this' (classical/poetic). رزم 'battle' is the first noun; وز is an archaic connective (here functioning like 'and' or linking 'battle' with 'our death'); مرگمان 'our death' is the second noun (مرگ + مان = 'our death'). چاره نیست is the predicative 'there is no remedy/solution.' The clause says there is no answer to this battle or to our dying.
segments.s086-g128: زمی is an adjective/noun 'earthly' or 'mortal' used as a substantive 'the mortal'; را is the object/topic marker (here introducing the thing being spoken about). جز 'except' + از گور 'from the grave' modifies گهواره 'cradle' and نیست closes the clause 'there is not'. Literal order gives 'For the mortal there is no cradle except from the grave' — idiomatically: 'There is no cradle for mortals but the grave.'
segments.s086-g145: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' (not the numeral). جادوی ساخت is an object (جادو, 'sorcery') + past verb ساخت 'made/performed', an archaic-poetic order where the action follows the thing done: 'performed magic'. با من به جنگ is a circumstantial phrase 'against me in battle' (با + me + به جنگ = 'with me into fight/against me').
segments.s086-g146: که introduces a result/clause ('so that/that'); با چشم روشن is an instrumental/experiential phrase 'to/for the clear/bright eye' (چشم روشن = clear sight). نماند is past negative 'did not remain' (3sg), taking آب و رنگ ('water and colour') as its objects — a poetic way to say sight of moisture/colour was lost.
segments.s086-g147: شب آمد is a simple past clause 'night came'. جهان سر به سر is a fixed adverbial 'the world, completely / all over' (سر به سر = entirely). تیره گشت uses گشت (past of شدن/گشتن) with the adjective تیره: 'became dark.'
segments.s086-g148: مرا بازو should be read as 'مرا بازو' = 'my arm' (مرا marking possession/affected person before the noun in older poetic usage). از کوفتن is an ablative gerund phrase 'from striking/striking repeatedly', and خیره گشت is a predicate 'became stunned/benumbed' (خیره here = astonished/dazed), with گشت = 'became'.
segments.s086-g149: تو گفتی is an address + past verb 'you said'. زمانه is the subject 'the age / the times'. سرآید here has the idiomatic sense 'to end/cease' (سرآید زمانه = 'the age will end'). همی is an archaic enclitic adding emphasis/assurance ('indeed' / 'surely').
segments.s086-g150: هوا is used metaphorically as 'desire/passion' here. زیر خاک اندر آید is a verbal clause 'goes/comes under the earth' (زیر = under, خاک = earth, اندر آید = archaic 'enters/comes into'), and the final همی again adds emphasis. Word order is archaic: prepositional phrase + اندر آید (verb).
segments.s086-g151: ببایست is an archaic/modal past-form 'it was necessary / one must', followed by the infinitive برگشتن 'to return/withdraw'. از رزمگاه marks the source/place 'from the battlefield'. Together they express obligation to retreat.
segments.s086-g152: که introduces the explanatory/reason clause ('for/that'). گرد سپه بود is elliptical/poetic — گرد here conveys the sense of a mass/gathering/about (the army was round/assembled) and سپه is 'the host/army'; بود is the past copula 'was'. و شب شد سیاه is coordinate: 'and the night became black' (شد = became, سیاه = dark).
